content.text: Why Conflict Is Inevitable in Enterprise Claude Programmes

A Claude programme that introduces new capabilities, changes how data is accessed, and produces clear ROI creates conditions for conflict. BUs compete for build slots. Data owners resist sharing data with adjacent teams. Compliance functions dispute what the security team has already approved. Middle managers who did not ask for AI tools receive pressure to adopt them. These conflicts are not programme failures — they are signs that the programme matters enough for people to fight over.

The programme team's role is not to eliminate conflict but to resolve it quickly, at the lowest possible escalation level, using agreed governance mechanisms rather than informal power dynamics. Conflicts that are avoided accumulate as unresolved tension and then surface at the worst possible moment — during a gate review, at the point of pilot launch, or after a use case goes live. Unresolved conflict is the leading cause of enterprise AI programme delays that were not predicted in the project plan.
Anatomy of a Claude Programme Conflict

Most conflicts in Claude programmes have a three-layer structure: the position (what each party says they want), the interest (why they want it), and the constraint (what is limiting the available options). Effective resolution requires addressing the interest and constraint layers, not just negotiating between positions.

Example: Two BUs dispute which one gets the shared Claude knowledge base to include their policy documentation first. Position A: 'Our documents should be prioritised because we have the most queries.' Position B: 'Our documents should be prioritised because our use case is more complex and needs the knowledge base sooner.' The conflict appears to be about queue order (positions). The underlying interests are about delivery timeline pressure and perceived programme fairness. The constraint is that the knowledge base ingestion pipeline can only process one document set per sprint.

Resolution that addresses positions only (whoever argues louder wins) produces a decision but damages the relationship. Resolution that addresses interests (both BUs are under timeline pressure; one is genuinely earlier in the delivery chain) and constraints (can the ingestion pipeline be parallelised, or can one BU start with a partial document set?) produces a sustainable outcome.
Conflict Types in Claude Programmes

attrs.alt: Four common Claude programme conflict types: scope, data access, priority, and governance conflicts

content.text: Scope Conflicts

Scope conflicts arise when the programme's defined boundaries do not align with what BUs expected or want. They commonly appear when a pilot succeeds and BUs request extensions before the programme has capacity to deliver them. A BU that was promised 'a customer query assistant' and discovers it will not cover internal HR queries during this programme cycle will resist this limitation — and if the limitation was never formally documented, the programme team has no clear authority to enforce it.

Prevention: a signed programme charter with an explicitly documented scope, and a formal change request process for scope additions. When a BU requests scope expansion, the change request documents what is being added, what is being deferred or deprioritised to accommodate it, and who must approve the trade-off. This transforms an informal request ('can we add X?') into a formal decision ('adding X requires deferring Y by 6 weeks — steering committee, do you approve?').
Data Access Conflicts

Data access conflicts occur when one BU needs data controlled by another BU and the data-owning BU is unwilling to share it on terms acceptable to both parties. The conflict is rarely about the data itself — it is about liability, competitive advantage, perceived workload (the data owner must do extra work to provide the data), and lack of trust in how the data will be used.

Resolution mechanism: a Data Sharing Agreement (DSA) that documents what data is shared, for what purpose, under what access controls, for how long, with what review cadence, and with what rights retained by the data owner. A DSA converts a trust problem into a contract problem — one that legal can arbitrate if necessary. Data owners are more willing to share when the terms are explicit and they retain control over the review-and-revoke mechanism.
Priority and Resource Conflicts

Priority conflicts arise when the programme has capacity to build one use case at a time but multiple BUs have approved use cases waiting for a build slot. These conflicts are usually between BU sponsors rather than between BUs and the programme team. They are low in direct harm but high in relationship damage — a BU that loses a priority dispute and feels the decision was unfair will be less cooperative in future programme interactions.

The priority scoring matrix described in the stakeholder mapping section is the primary resolution mechanism. When scores are genuinely equal, the steering committee makes the call. The key is that the decision is made transparently, the criteria are known in advance, and the losing BU is given a committed date for the next slot. A fair process that produces an unfavourable decision is far better received than an opaque one.
The Conflict Escalation Ladder

attrs.alt: Conflict escalation ladder with five levels from direct resolution to programme pause

content.text: All conflicts should start at Level 1 and only escalate when the lower level cannot reach resolution within its target timeframe. Skipping levels — going directly to the steering committee with something that should have been resolved bilaterally — is itself a governance failure that wastes senior leaders' time and signals poor programme management.
Level 1: Direct Resolution

Most conflicts can be resolved by the parties involved in a direct conversation facilitated by the programme delivery manager. The delivery manager's role at this level is process management, not arbitration: create the conversation, ensure both positions are heard, identify the interest and constraint layers, generate options, and confirm agreement. If agreement is reached, the delivery manager sends a brief email confirming what was agreed and to whom. No formal record is required beyond the email.

The target timeframe is 1–3 days. A conflict that has not reached Level 1 resolution within 3 days should be considered for escalation to Level 2. Allowing conflicts to sit unresolved for weeks at Level 1 is the most common governance failure — it signals that the delivery manager lacks either the facilitation skills or the authority to move things forward.
Level 2: Mediated Discussion

If direct resolution fails, the programme director chairs a structured 60-minute mediation session. The format is specific: each party has 10 minutes to present their position without interruption, then the session moves to interest clarification (what is behind the position?), then to option generation (what could both parties live with?), then to agreement or escalation.

The programme director is the mediator, not the decision-maker, at this level. Their role is to structure the conversation and help parties find a workable outcome — not to impose one. If the parties reach agreement, the programme director sends a formal meeting record to both parties and the steering committee for information. If they do not reach agreement within the 60-minute session, the conflict escalates to Level 3.
Level 3: Steering Committee Decision

Conflicts that reach Level 3 are brought to the programme steering committee for a binding decision. The programme delivery manager prepares a one-page conflict brief: the nature of the conflict, the positions of each party, the interests behind those positions, the options considered at Levels 1 and 2 and why they were not accepted, and a recommended resolution. The steering committee reviews the brief and makes a decision at their next scheduled meeting — or an emergency meeting if the conflict is blocking delivery.

The steering committee decision is binding. A BU sponsor who disagrees with the decision can note their disagreement formally, but the decision stands. A governance structure in which steering committee decisions can be relitigated informally ceases to function as a governance structure.
Level 4 and 5: Executive Resolution and Programme Pause

Level 4 escalation — to C-suite or board level — is rare and should be treated as a programme risk event. Its occurrence signals that the steering committee's authority was insufficient or that the conflict involves interests above the programme level (for example, a conflict between the Chief Data Officer and the Chief Risk Officer about data governance principles that the programme cannot resolve independently).

Level 5 — programme pause or scope reduction — is the correct response when a conflict is genuinely irresolvable within the current constraints. A use case that requires data sharing that the data owner categorically refuses, for legitimate governance reasons, should be descoped rather than forced through or allowed to block the rest of the programme. Descoping a blocked use case and defining conditions for re-entry is a governance decision, not a failure.
Preventing Conflict Through Governance Design
Many conflicts are prevented by governance design decisions made before the programme starts. Three prevention mechanisms:
Programme Charter with Explicit Scope

A programme charter signed by all BU sponsors at programme launch documents: the programme's objectives, the scope (what use cases are in scope, what are explicitly out of scope), the governance structure (who sits on the steering committee, what decisions they have authority to make), the escalation path for conflicts, and the criteria for use case prioritisation. A charter converts implicit assumptions into explicit agreements — and provides the reference point for resolving later disputes about what was 'agreed' at the start.
RACI for Every Programme Decision

A RACI matrix (Responsible, Accountable, Consulted, Informed) that covers the key programme decisions eliminates the most common source of governance conflict — ambiguity about who has decision authority. Critical RACI items for a Claude programme:
Decision
Responsible
Accountable
Consulted
Informed
Add new use case to scope
Delivery Manager
Programme Director
BU Sponsors, Solution Architect
Steering Committee
Approve data sharing between BUs
Data Owner + Requester
Chief Data Officer
Legal, Compliance, IT Security
Programme Director
Gate 4 funding approval
Programme Director
Steering Committee Chair
Finance, BU Sponsors
All stakeholders
Choose integration architecture (Pattern A/B/C/D)
Solution Architect
Platform Engineering Lead
BU Tech Lead, Programme Director
Steering Committee
Defer or reject a use case at gate
Gate Owner
Gate Decision Authority
Risk, Compliance (if relevant)
BU Sponsor, Programme Director
Scale pilot to full rollout
Delivery Manager
Steering Committee
BU Lead, Finance, IT
All programme stakeholders
'Accountable' must have exactly one name per decision — if two people are Accountable for the same decision, neither is. RACI ambiguity is the root cause of most governance conflicts.
Conflict Register

A conflict register is a shared document that logs every active conflict, its level on the escalation ladder, the parties involved, the current status, and the target resolution date. It serves two functions: it makes conflicts visible (preventing the common pattern of conflicts being managed informally and surfacing as surprises at gate reviews), and it creates accountability for resolution (a conflict with a named owner and a deadline is more likely to be resolved than one that is 'being worked on').

# Conflict register data model
# Track active conflicts and their escalation status

conflict_register = [
    {
        "id": "CONF-003",
        "raised_date": "2025-04-10",
        "conflict_type": "data_access",
        "description": "Finance BU (data owner) will not share GL transaction data with Operations BU for spend analysis use case without approved DSA",
        "parties": ["Finance BU — Jo Meredith", "Operations BU — Sam Okafor"],
        "level": 2,  # Current escalation level
        "owner": "Programme Director — Alex Huang",
        "target_resolution_date": "2025-04-24",
        "status": "in_progress",
        "actions": [
            {"date": "2025-04-10", "action": "Level 1 conversation facilitated by Delivery Manager", "outcome": "No agreement — Finance requires DSA before any sharing"},
            {"date": "2025-04-14", "action": "Level 2 mediation session scheduled for 2025-04-18"},
        ],
        "resolution": None,
    },
    {
        "id": "CONF-004",
        "raised_date": "2025-04-12",
        "conflict_type": "priority",
        "description": "HR and Legal both requesting next available build slot. HR has higher priority score (3.8 vs 3.2) but Legal claims urgent regulatory driver",
        "parties": ["HR BU — Pat Chen", "Legal BU — Sam Williams"],
        "level": 3,  # Escalated to steering committee
        "owner": "Steering Committee",
        "target_resolution_date": "2025-04-28",
        "status": "escalated",
        "actions": [
            {"date": "2025-04-12", "action": "Level 1 attempt; Legal contested priority score methodology"},
            {"date": "2025-04-15", "action": "Level 2 mediation; Legal presented regulatory timeline; no agreement reached"},
            {"date": "2025-04-16", "action": "Conflict brief submitted to steering committee for April 28 meeting"},
        ],
        "resolution": None,
    },
]

def active_conflicts(register):
    return [c for c in register if c["resolution"] is None]

def overdue_conflicts(register, today="2025-04-20"):
    return [
        c for c in active_conflicts(register)
        if c["target_resolution_date"] < today
    ]

print(f"Active conflicts: {len(active_conflicts(conflict_register))}")
print(f"Overdue: {len(overdue_conflicts(conflict_register))}")
for c in active_conflicts(conflict_register):
    print(f"  [{c['id']}] Level {c['level']}: {c['conflict_type']} — {c['status']}")

# Active conflicts: 2
# Overdue: 0
#   [CONF-003] Level 2: data_access — in_progress
#   [CONF-004] Level 3: priority — escalated
Specific Conflict Scenarios and Responses
CISO vs. Business Sponsor: Security vs. Speed

The most common and most disruptive conflict in enterprise Claude programmes is between the CISO (who requires thorough security review before approval) and the business sponsor (who has committed to a delivery date and is under pressure to show results). This conflict is not about personalities — it is a structural tension between security governance timelines and business delivery expectations.

The effective response is not to choose between speed and security, but to provide the CISO with the specific information required to make a security decision as quickly as possible. A CISO who says 'I need to review this properly' is often expressing 'I do not have the information I need to make a risk decision.' A pre-populated security assessment template — covering data flows, API security controls, access management, data retention, and compliance obligations — transforms an open-ended review into a checklist that can be completed in a defined timeframe.

Never position security review as a blocker to be overcome. Position it as a gate to be passed with evidence. The CISO who is given excellent evidence approves quickly. The CISO who is pressured without evidence digs in.
Data Owner vs. Use Case Team: Access Terms

A data owner who refuses to share data is usually expressing one of four concerns: liability (if Claude produces a bad output from my data, who is responsible?), workload (I have to do extra work to extract and format this data), quality (my data has known quality issues that I don't want to be blamed for), or competition (my BU's data is a competitive asset I don't want to share). Each concern has a specific response:
Liability:
DSA with explicit liability allocation. The use case team accepts responsibility for outputs produced from the data; the data owner is not liable for downstream use.
Workload:
The programme team provides data engineering resource to do the extraction and formatting — the data owner reviews and approves but does not do the work.
Quality:
Quality issues are documented in the Data Readiness Assessment; the use case team accepts known limitations explicitly rather than discovering them in build.
Competition:
Limiting data access to approved use cases with read-only access controlled by the data owner; audit logging of all queries against the data.
Two BU Sponsors in Competition

When two BU sponsors are competing for the same resource (build slot, developer time, platform capacity), the conflict often becomes personal if it is handled informally. The sponsor who loses feels that the other sponsor 'won' due to relationship rather than merit. The programme must depersonalise the decision by making the prioritisation criteria and scores visible to both sponsors before the decision is made. A sponsor who loses on a transparent merit-based decision is disappointed; one who loses on an opaque informal decision is antagonistic.
Facilitation Skills for the Delivery Manager

The delivery manager is the first-line conflict resolver in a Claude programme. Effective facilitation at Level 1 prevents most conflicts from reaching the steering committee. These skills are practical and learnable — they do not require formal mediation training, but they do require deliberate practice.
Separating Positions from Interests

The most valuable facilitation move is the 'why' question: 'I understand you want X. Help me understand why X matters to you — what would having X allow you to do, and what problem does not having X create?' Asking this question of both parties, and genuinely listening to the answers, often reveals that the underlying interests are compatible even when the stated positions are not.

Example: BU A's position is 'we need the knowledge base deployed by April 30.' BU B's position is 'we need more time for quality review before deployment.' These positions conflict. But the interests behind them may be: BU A is trying to hit a board presentation date in May; BU B has a specific concern about three document types that need additional review. The resolution is obvious once interests are clear: deploy the knowledge base by April 30 with a flag on the three document types that are still under review, and communicate the limitation in the launch communication.
Generating Options Without Advocating

A delivery manager who advocates for a specific resolution introduces their own preferences into the conflict, which undermines the neutrality required for both parties to trust the process. The facilitation approach is to generate multiple options and test each against the interests of both parties:
Option generation:
'What are three things that could work for you, even if they're not your first preference?' Applied to both parties, this generates 4–8 options, at least some of which will be acceptable to both.
Interest testing:
'If we did X, what specifically would not work for you? What about Y?' This moves the conversation from 'is this acceptable?' (binary) to 'what would need to change about this option?' (generative).
Time-boxing:

'We have 45 minutes. Let's see if we can find something that works, and if we can't, we'll escalate — so the worst case is the steering committee makes the decision for us.' This reduces the stakes of the conversation and makes exploration feel safer.
When to Stop Facilitating and Escalate
A delivery manager who keeps trying to resolve a conflict that should be escalated loses time and credibility. Escalation triggers:
The conflict has been in Level 1 for more than 3 days without movement
One party has stopped engaging with the facilitation process (non-responses to meeting requests, going around the process informally)
The conflict involves a decision that is formally above the delivery manager's authority (for example, data sharing arrangements that require legal sign-off)
The conflict has become personal — parties are attacking each other rather than discussing the issue

Escalating early is not a failure of facilitation. A delivery manager who escalates a conflict after two unsuccessful direct resolution attempts, with a clear brief for the programme director, is managing the process correctly. A delivery manager who lets a conflict drag for weeks at Level 1 because they are reluctant to 'escalate' is allowing the conflict to compound.
Building Conflict Resilience into Programme Culture
Conflict-resilient programme cultures are ones where people feel safe raising disagreements early, before they become hardened positions. Three practices build this culture:
Retrospectives That Include Governance Issues

Post-gate retrospectives and quarterly programme reviews should explicitly ask: 'What conflicts or tensions came up in this period? How were they handled? What would we do differently?' Making conflict discussion a normal part of programme rhythm signals that surfacing disagreements is valued, not penalised. Programmes where this question is never asked tend to have conflicts that go underground and surface as resignations, escalations, or post-programme complaints.
Named Conflict Resolution Champions

Designate one member of the programme team as the conflict resolution contact — the person anyone in the programme can approach informally with a concern before it becomes a formal conflict. This person does not have formal authority; their role is to listen, help the person articulate their concern, and advise on whether and how to raise it formally. Having a named individual for this role lowers the threshold for early disclosure of concerns.
Acknowledging Resolved Conflicts

When a conflict is resolved — particularly at Level 2 or above — acknowledge it formally in the next programme update: 'A disagreement between Finance and Operations about data sharing terms was resolved this month. Both BUs signed a Data Sharing Agreement that allows the spending analysis use case to proceed.' This signals to the programme community that conflicts are normal, manageable, and do not end careers. It also provides social proof that the governance process works.
Conflict in Multi-Organisation Programmes

When a Claude programme spans multiple legal entities — a corporate parent and subsidiaries, a consortium of organisations, or a client-supplier programme — conflict governance becomes more complex. Several additional dimensions apply:
Jurisdictional Conflicts

Multi-organisation programmes may involve parties operating under different legal jurisdictions with different data protection laws, employment regulations, or procurement rules. A conflict over data sharing between a UK entity (subject to UK GDPR) and a German entity (subject to BDSG as well as EU GDPR) requires legal expertise from both jurisdictions, not just one. Escalation in these cases should involve legal counsel from all relevant jurisdictions before a decision is made.
Inter-Organisational Decision Authority

In a consortium or multi-party programme, the steering committee must include representation from all parties, and the escalation path must have agreed authority at the top. A Level 4 escalation in a single-organisation programme goes to the CTO; in a multi-organisation programme, the equivalent level must be defined in the programme governance agreement — often a joint steering board with representatives of all parties and a tie-breaking mechanism (typically the lead organisation's representative or an independent chair).
Exit Clauses for Irresolvable Conflicts

Multi-organisation programme agreements should include explicit exit clauses that define how a party can withdraw from the programme without harming the remaining parties, and what happens to jointly developed assets (knowledge bases, fine-tuned models, shared infrastructure) in the event of a party's exit. Defining these terms before the programme starts is much easier than defining them during a conflict.
Measuring Conflict Management Effectiveness
A conflict management process that is working should produce measurable outcomes:
Metric
Target
Warning Signal
Average time to Level 1 resolution
≤ 3 days
> 5 days — facilitation not working or escalation too slow
Proportion of conflicts resolved at Level 1
< 50% — too many conflicts reaching formal governance; prevention mechanisms needed
Proportion escalating to Level 3+
> 20% — steering committee overloaded with conflicts; Level 2 mediation not effective
Conflicts unresolved after target date
Any — named conflicts with missed resolution dates must be escalated or formally extended
Recurring conflicts on the same issue
Any — same conflict re-emerging means the root resolution was not durable; re-examine

Track these metrics in the quarterly programme review. A programme with consistently low Level 1 resolution rates and high Level 3+ escalation rates has a governance design problem — either the escalation structure is not functioning, or the programme lacks the facilitation capacity to resolve conflicts early. Identify the pattern before it compounds.

Conflict management is a programme health metric, not a programme failure metric. A programme with zero conflicts is either very small or has suppressed disagreements rather than resolved them. The goal is fast, fair resolution at the lowest possible level — not the absence of conflict.
Summary

Conflict in enterprise Claude programmes is inevitable — it arises from competing interests in limited resources, data ownership disputes, security vs. speed tensions, and scope ambiguity. The programme team's role is to resolve conflicts quickly using agreed governance mechanisms: direct resolution first, then mediated discussion, then steering committee decision, then executive escalation, and finally programme pause or scope reduction if the conflict proves irresolvable.

Prevention mechanisms — a signed programme charter, a RACI covering all key decisions, a conflict register with named owners and target dates — reduce both the frequency and the severity of conflicts. Specific conflict types (scope, data, priority, governance) each have characteristic resolution approaches. The rule that applies in every case: address the interest and constraint layers, not just the surface positions, and keep the process visible, fair, and consistently applied.
